\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Given here, threshold wavelength λth=5500 Å=550 nm

For photoelectric emission, the wavelength of the light should be less than 550 nm.

Wavelength of infrared radiation extends from 700 nm-1 mm and that of ultraviolet radiation is 100-400 nm.

Since, UV light has less wavelength than 550 nm, so only UV would be able to emit photoelectrons.

subject: Physics
solution:
The force experienced by the electron in the electric field is given by $\vec{F} = q\vec{E}$.

Since the charge of an electron is $-e$ and the electric field is $\vec{E} = -2E_0\hat{i}$, the force is:

$\vec{F} = (-e)(-2E_0\hat{i}) = 2eE_0\hat{i}$

The acceleration of the electron is:

$\vec{a} = \dfrac{\vec{F}}{m} = \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}\hat{i}$

Using the first equation of motion, the velocity of the electron at time $t$ is:

$\vec{v}(t) = \vec{V} + \vec{a}t = \left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)\hat{i}$

The de Broglie wavelength of the electron at time $t$ is:

$\lambda = \dfrac{h}{m|\vec{v}(t)|} = \dfrac{h}{m\left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)}$

We are given that $\lambda_0 = \dfrac{h}{4mv_0}$, which implies $h = 4mv_0\lambda_0$. Substituting this into the expression for $\lambda$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{4mv_0\lambda_0}{m\left(v_0 + \dfrac{2eE_0}{m}t\right)}$

Dividing the numerator and the denominator by $mv_0$, we get:

$\lambda = \dfrac{4\lambda_0}{1 + \dfrac{2E_0 e}{m}\dfrac{t}{v_0}}$

Answer: $\dfrac{4\lambda_0}{\left[1 + \dfrac{2E_0 e}{m}\dfrac{t}{v_0}\right]}$

subject: Physics
solution:
De Broglie wavelength of proton \& electron $=\lambda$
$\begin{aligned} & \because \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{h}}{\mathrm{p}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{p}_{\text {proton }}=\mathrm{p}_{\text {electron }} \\ & \because \mathrm{KE}=\frac{\mathrm{p}^2}{2 \mathrm{~m}} \\ & \therefore \mathrm{KE}_{\text {proton }} < \mathrm{KE}_{\text {electron }} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{p}} < \mathrm{K}_{\mathrm{c}}\right]}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \Phi + eV_0$

The energy of the incident photon is:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{331 \times 10^{-9}}$

$E = \dfrac{19.86 \times 10^{-26}}{331 \times 10^{-9}} = 6 \times 10^{-19}$ J

The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is:

$K_{\text{max}} = eV_0 = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.2 = 0.32 \times 10^{-19}$ J

The work function $\Phi$ is:

$\Phi = E - K_{\text{max}}$

$\Phi = 6 \times 10^{-19} - 0.32 \times 10^{-19} = 5.68 \times 10^{-19}$ J

Comparing with $\alpha \times 10^{-19}$ J, we get $\alpha = 5.68$.

Answer: $5.68$

subject: Physics

KE=qV

Using relation between momentum and kinetic energy

⇒p22 m=qV⇒p=2mqV

We know that momentum of charged particle is p=hλ.

Thus, λ=h2mqV

Now, λpλd=mdVdmpVp

⇒12=2VdVp⇒VpVd=4

subject: Physics
solution:
We know that wavelength is inversely proportional to momentum, let λB is de-Broglie Wavelength corresponds to momentum PB, similarly  let λG is Wavelength at ground state corresponds to momentumPG, 
λBλG= PGPB= mvGmvB, and velocity, V∝zn, where Z is the atomic number, and n is a number of orbits, here, nG=1 (ground state), nB=3 (second excited state). So, λBλG= nBnG= 31⇒λB=3λG

subject: Physics
solution:
Using Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$eV_s = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

For the first case:

$3eV_0 = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

For the second case:

$eV_0 = \dfrac{hc}{2\lambda} - \Phi$

Multiplying the second equation by $3$ and equating with the first equation:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi = \dfrac{3hc}{2\lambda} - 3\Phi$

$2\Phi = \dfrac{3hc}{2\lambda} - \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

$2\Phi = \dfrac{hc}{2\lambda}$

$\Phi = \dfrac{hc}{4\lambda}$

Since the work function $\Phi = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_0}$, where $\lambda_0$ is the threshold wavelength:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda_0} = \dfrac{hc}{4\lambda}$

$\lambda_0 = 4\lambda$

Given $\lambda_0 = \alpha\lambda$, we get $\alpha = 4$.

Answer: $4$
